claims.text: Some scientists believe time is non-linear and that past, present and future coexist in parallel dimensions; when those dimensions collide, the resulting tear in spacetime can momentarily transport a person to another time.

claims.rationale: The framing is the host stating the central physical premise of the episode. Some physicists do entertain block-universe and many-worlds interpretations, but the specific claim that colliding parallel realities tear spacetime and beam people across time is not supported by mainstream physics; it is the speculative scaffold the host uses to motivate the rest of the episode.

claims.text: In 1901 two Oxford academics, Charlotte Moberly and Eleanor Jourdain, walked the grounds of Versailles and were transported back to roughly 1792, encountering 18th-century courtiers and a woman they later identified from portraits as Marie Antoinette.

claims.rationale: The Moberly-Jourdain incident is a real and famous case in psychical research literature; both women genuinely believed something paranormal happened. However, the host himself returns in the verdict to note their notes did not match for three months, the year shifted between 1789 and 1792, Marie Antoinette was not at Versailles in 1792, and the Society for Psychical Research dismissed the case. directTruth is low: the event happened in some form, but the time-slip interpretation is unsupported.

claims.text: Carl Jung experienced a time slip in early-1930s Ravenna, Italy, where he and a companion vividly observed and later described in detail four mosaics in the Baptistery of Neon that had actually been destroyed by fire centuries earlier.

evidence.quote:
in the early 1930s K young visited Rena

one of the city's most religious

claims.rationale: Jung did write about a strange experience in Ravenna in his autobiography Memories, Dreams, Reflections, where he and Toni Wolff recalled mosaics that did not exist as he remembered them. But the host directly debunks this in the verdict: Jung was in a different church, the story is secondhand, and the dates and locations are confused. The romantic reading of it as a literal time slip is not warranted by the historical record.

claims.text: In 1979 two British couples, the Simpsons and the Gisbys, drove through France, stayed at a strangely outdated and inexpensive hotel lit only by oil lamps, and on a return trip could not find the hotel; even their photographs of it were missing without leaving blanks on the negatives.

claims.rationale: The 1979 Gisby/Simpson vanishing-hotel story circulates widely on paranormal sites but the host correctly highlights the obvious problems in the verdict: a 1979 Chrysler would have alarmed villagers in the early 1900s, and 1970s francs would not have been spendable. The story has shifted over the years and has no documentary corroboration.

claims.rationale: The host explicitly debunks this in the verdict section: no such person ever existed and every source for the story is a paranormal aggregator copying the same tale. The cinematic detail of running into a former regular customer the host calls out as too convenient.

claims.text: In 1953 a young plumber's apprentice named Harry Martindale, working in the basement of the Treasurer's House in York, watched a column of Roman soldiers march straight through the wall and across the floor visible only from the knees up, and decades later excavation revealed an ancient Roman road eighteen inches below the basement floor that exactly explained the missing legs and confirmed historical details he could not have known.

of the treasurer's house in New York

claims.rationale: Harry Martindale's 1953 Treasurer's House sighting is a genuinely well-known and unusually detailed paranormal report, and the post-1990s excavations of a Roman road beneath the building are real. The host acknowledges in the verdict that he wants to believe this one but that Martindale only told the story years later and the details shifted over time. The Roman road and York legion presence are documented historical facts; the literal apparition of marching soldiers is not.

claims.rationale: The Liverpool / Bold Street time slip canon is genuinely a popular folk corpus with online communities collecting reports. The host himself concludes in the verdict that there is not a shred of evidence for any of the specific stories, all sources are other websites recycling the same tales, and Mothercare never had a Bold Street location. Frank is the only case with a partly corroborating witness (Julie French) and even there the host says it comes down to whether you believe her.

claims.text: Liverpool time slips can be explained by telluric currents - naturally occurring earth-surface electrical currents - interacting with the high-voltage rails of the Liverpool underground arranged in concentric circles centered on Bold Street, producing time vortices.

claims.rationale: Telluric currents are a real geophysical phenomenon, and electromagnetic effects on biological systems are studied. But the host himself debunks this later: the Liverpool underground is not actually concentric, the schematic map just looks that way for legibility, and Brooks alley is not even inside the only real loop. The leap from real telluric currents to time-bending vortices is unsupported.

claims.text: Ley lines - hypothetical energy lines connecting sacred sites - converge near Bold Street, with over thirty historic locations' ley lines running through Liverpool's time-slip hot zone, suggesting ancient peoples deliberately built sacred sites where telluric energy was strongest.

claims.rationale: Ley lines are a 1920s idea by Alfred Watkins later picked up by the New Age movement; they are not a recognized scientific concept and the apparent alignment of historic sites is widely considered a statistical artifact (any dense set of points produces apparent lines). The host even rebuts himself moments later, noting the lay-line/telluric story doesn't sound like a very scientific way to explain time slips.

claims.text: Einstein's relativity establishes that time is not constant - it dilates with speed and gravity - so unknown forces capable of causing extreme time dilation could in principle produce time slips, and wormholes (Einstein-Rosen bridges) could likewise allow movement between different points in time.

claims.rationale: The first half is mainstream physics: time dilation is real and measured (GPS satellites must correct for it). Wormhole solutions to Einstein's field equations exist mathematically. The leap to ordinary humans walking through micro-wormholes on a Liverpool street is not licensed by the math; traversable wormholes would require exotic negative-energy matter not known to exist. directTruth is moderate because the physics premise is solid even if the time-slip extrapolation is not.

claims.text: Time slips may be macroscopic quantum events at the boundary between parallel universes (the many-worlds interpretation), with time-symmetry and retrocausality letting past, present and future influence each other so that occasional reality-collisions strand people in another era.

claims.rationale: The many-worlds interpretation, time-symmetric formulations of quantum mechanics, and retrocausal interpretations are real research programs in the foundations of physics, but they describe quantum-scale phenomena. There is no accepted mechanism by which decoherence at the quantum level scales up to bridge entire macroscopic realities for human observers. Alberto Miatello (cited in the script) is not a mainstream physicist and the 2018 paper is self-published.

claims.text: On reflection, the Versailles 1901 case has issues that fatally undercut its credibility: Moberly and Jourdain's notes did not match for three months, kept changing across editions, the year drifted between 1789 and 1792, and crucially Marie Antoinette could not have been at Versailles in 1792 because she was already imprisoned at the Tuileries until August of that year.

ring true I don't believe

claims.rationale: The host walks through specific concrete reasons: the three-month gap before comparing notes (memories drift), conflicting witness reports, the book's many editions adding details, and the historical fact that Marie Antoinette was at Tuileries / in prison from October 1789 onward. These are sound critical points consistent with the actual scholarly debunking by E. H. Sewell and others.

dependencies.rationale: directly rebuts the literal time-slip reading of the Moberly-Jourdain incident

claims.text: Carl Jung's Ravenna mosaic story is debunked: it is all secondhand, it confuses dates and locations, and Jung was in fact in a different church that did have mosaics like the ones he remembered.

claims.rationale: Critical reviewers of Jung's Ravenna anecdote (e.g. Sonu Shamdasani) have noted exactly the problems the host names: Jung describes the experience decades later, the architectural details correspond to other Ravenna sites with mosaics that do exist, and the surviving record is thin. The host's dismissal is well-grounded.

claims.text: The Sebastian Reigns New York diner story is fabricated: no such person ever existed and every source is a paranormal website recycling the same tale.

claims.rationale: The host's critique - the absence of any primary source and the way paranormal sites recycle each other's stories without verification - is exactly the right epistemic standard for evaluating internet-folklore time-slip narratives. The story has no documented witness, no original venue, and no corroborating record.

claims.text: Liverpool is genuinely Ground Zero for time-slip lore but none of the specific Bold Street stories can be verified - Frank/Julie is the only case with even a single named witness, Shawn's shoplifter story keeps growing, and the Mothercare on Bold Street never existed (Mothercare did have other Liverpool shops, but not on Bold Street).

claims.rationale: The verdict's methodology - checking primary sources, looking for named witnesses, checking whether named businesses existed at the named addresses - is exactly how this kind of folklore should be evaluated, and lands on the right answer for each case. The lone partial corroborator (Julie French) gets credit but appropriately bounded.

claims.text: Most time-slip experiences are made up, but we cannot rule them all out because we do not yet understand enough about how the universe works to declare them impossible - the host wants to believe time slips are real even after debunking the specific cases.

evidence.quote:
most time
slip experiences are made up but I can't
say all of them are we just don't know
enough about how our universe works to
say Time Slips aren't real I want to
believe they are I love the idea of
watching the present dissolve 50 or 100
years into the past that reminds me
today we only talked about Time Slips to
the past there are a lot of stories

claims.rationale: This is a calibrated agnostic stance after the debunking: high confidence the existing reports are folklore, but appropriate humility about open questions in physics. It is an opinion claim about epistemic posture rather than a falsifiable empirical proposition.
